content:
# Acts Chapter 11 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 238-239 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_011_page_001.jpg, ACT_011_page_002.jpg

---

**1** Now the apostles and the brothers who were throughout Judea heard that the Gentiles also had received the word of God.

**2** And when Peter went up to Jerusalem, those of the circumcision contended with him,

**3** saying, "You went in to uncircumcised men and ate with them."

**4** But Peter began and explained it to them in order, saying,

**5** "I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision: a certain vessel descending like a great sheet, let down from heaven by its four corners, and it came to me.

**6** Upon which, gazing intently, I observed and saw the four-footed creatures of the earth, and the wild beasts, and the creeping things, and the birds of the heaven.

**7** And I also heard a voice saying to me, 'Rise, Peter; kill and eat.'

**8** But I said, 'By no means, Lord, for nothing common or unclean has ever entered into my mouth.'

**9** But a voice answered a second time from heaven, 'What God has cleansed, you must not call common.'

**10** And this happened three times, and all was drawn up again into heaven.

**11** And behold, at that very moment three men stood before the house in which we were, having been sent from Caesarea to me.

**12** And the Spirit told me to go with them, making no distinction. And these six brothers also came with me, and we entered into the man's house.

**13** And he reported to us how he had seen the angel standing in his house and saying, 'Send to Joppa and summon Simon who is called Peter,

**14** who will speak words to you by which you shall be saved, you and all your household.'

**15** And as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell upon them, just as upon us also in the beginning.

**16** And I remembered the word of the Lord, how he said, 'John indeed baptized with water, but you shall be baptized with the Holy Spirit.'

**17** If therefore God gave to them the same gift as also to us who believed upon the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I to be able to hinder God?"

**18** And when they heard these things, they fell silent and glorified God, saying, "Then to the Gentiles also God has granted repentance unto life."

**19** Now those who had been scattered because of the tribulation that arose over Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia and Cyprus and Antioch, speaking the word to no one except Jews only.

**20** But there were some of them, men of Cyprus and Cyrene, who, when they came to Antioch, spoke also to the Greeks, proclaiming the good news of the Lord Jesus.

**21** And the hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number who believed turned to the Lord.

**22** And the report concerning them came to the ears of the church in Jerusalem, and they sent out Barnabas as far as Antioch.

**23** Who, when he arrived and saw the grace of God, rejoiced and exhorted them all to remain with the Lord with resolute heart.

**24** For he was a good man, and full of the Holy Spirit and of faith. And a considerable crowd was added to the Lord.

**25** And he departed to Tarsus to seek out Saul,

**26** and when he had found him, he brought him to Antioch. And it came to pass that for a whole year they were gathered together with the church and taught a considerable crowd. And the disciples were first called Christians in Antioch.

**27** Now in these days prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch.

**28** And one of them, named Agabus, stood up and indicated through the Spirit that a great famine was about to come upon the whole inhabited world — which came to pass under Claudius.

**29** And each of the disciples, according as anyone had the means, determined to send relief to the brothers dwelling in Judea —

**30** which also they did, sending it to the elders through the hand of Barnabas and Saul.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Textual Observations from Codex Sinaiticus

1. **Verse 2 — "those of the circumcision" (οἱ ἐκ περιτομῆς):** Sinaiticus preserves this phrase identifying the faction within the Jerusalem church that challenged Peter. The formal rendering retains the partitive construction to show these were a defined group, not the entire community.

2. **Verse 5 — Vision description:** Sinaiticus reads the vision account with close verbal agreement to the narrative in Acts 10, though Peter's recounting naturally varies in minor details. The vessel (σκεῦος) described "like a great sheet" (ὡς ὀθόνην μεγάλην) is rendered formally to preserve the simile.

3. **Verse 12 — "making no distinction" (μηδὲν διακρίναντα):** This participle carries the sense of "without hesitating" or "without making a distinction." The formal rendering preserves the theological weight — the Spirit commanded Peter not to differentiate between Jew and Gentile.

4. **Verse 14 — "you shall be saved" (σωθήσῃ):** Sinaiticus includes the full soteriological promise — words by which "you and all your household" shall be saved. This extends the scope of salvation beyond the individual to the entire household, a significant Lukan theme.

5. **Verse 17 — "who was I to be able to hinder God?":** Peter's rhetorical question (τίς ἤμην δυνατὸς κωλῦσαι τὸν θεόν) is rendered with formal precision. The construction emphasizes Peter's inability (δυνατός) to stand against divine action.

6. **Verse 20 — "Greeks" (Ἕλληνας):** Sinaiticus reads Ἕλληνας ("Greeks," i.e., Greek-speaking Gentiles) rather than the variant Ἑλληνιστάς ("Hellenists," i.e., Greek-speaking Jews). This reading is critical, as it marks the decisive expansion of the gospel to full Gentiles in Antioch — a theological turning point in Luke's narrative.

7. **Verse 22 — Barnabas sent to Antioch:** The Jerusalem church dispatches Barnabas (not as an inspector but as an encourager), reflecting the apostolic community's response to reports of Gentile conversion. His characterization in verse 24 as "a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and of faith" is unique Lukan commendation.

8. **Verse 26 — "Christians" (Χριστιανούς):** Sinaiticus preserves the first occurrence of this designation. The passive construction χρηματίσαι suggests the name was given to the disciples by outsiders in Antioch, though it may also carry the sense of divine appointment (the verb can mean "to bear a name by divine direction").

9. **Verse 28 — "the whole inhabited world" (ὅλην τὴν οἰκουμένην):** The term οἰκουμένη refers to the Roman world. The famine under Claudius is historically attested and provides one of Luke's chronological anchors.

10. **Verse 30 — "elders" (πρεσβυτέρους):** This is the first mention of elders in the Jerusalem church in Acts, marking a development in ecclesial structure. Relief is sent through Barnabas and Saul, establishing the pattern of Antioch's material support for Jerusalem.

### Translation Philosophy

This formal equivalence rendering maintains close fidelity to the Greek word order and syntactic structures of Sinaiticus where English permits, while ensuring readability. Peter's speech (vv. 5-17) is rendered as direct discourse preserving his first-person recounting. The chapter records two pivotal developments in early Christianity: the Jerusalem church's acceptance of Gentile inclusion (vv. 1-18) and the emergence of Antioch as a major center of the faith (vv. 19-30).

— **LOGOS**
